1.synthesis: "Soon afterward he went on through cities and villages, proclaiming and bringing the good news of the kingdom of God. And the twelve were with him, and also some women who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities: Mary, called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out, and Joanna, the wife of Chuza, Herod's household manager, and Susanna, and many others, who provided for them out of their means" (vv.1–3). The brief note is unique to Luke and is of considerable historical significance: it names women as members of Jesus's traveling community, identifies them by prior experience of healing, and specifies their financial support of the mission.
1.historical_context: Named women disciples traveling with a rabbi and the Twelve was highly unusual in first-century Jewish culture; rabbis typically taught male students only, and mixed-gender traveling groups would have been socially noted. Mary Magdalene's identification as the one from whom seven demons were cast is consistent with Luke 7:36–50's previous focus on a woman sinner (though not identified as the same person); her name heads all Gospel lists of the women disciples. Joanna is named as the wife of Chuza, the manager of Herod Antipas's household — a connection to the court of the very ruler who imprisoned John the Baptist and who Jesus calls "that fox" (13:32). That a courtier's wife was among Jesus's followers suggests the mission reached across social boundaries in both directions: poor and wealthy, sinners and Herodian.
1.literary_structure: The note on the women's support serves a practical and theological function. Practically, it explains how Jesus and the Twelve sustained themselves during the itinerant mission. Theologically, it establishes these women as witnesses of the whole mission from Galilee forward — their role as Easter witnesses (they go to the tomb, they see the risen Lord, they tell the Twelve) is grounded in their prior presence throughout the ministry. Luke explicitly connects the women at the Galilean mission (8:2–3) with the women at the cross (23:49), the burial (23:55–56), and the empty tomb (24:1–11).
1.theological_application: The women's provision for the mission "out of their means" (ek tōn hyparchontōn autais) is the first use of the wealth theme that will dominate Luke 12–16. Wealth used in support of the kingdom's mission is wealth put to its proper use; the women demonstrate the liberality that the Rich Fool (12:13–21), the Unjust Steward (16:1–13), and the Rich Man and Lazarus (16:19–31) will all misuse or miss. The diversity of the women's backgrounds (Mary Magdalene: healed of demonic oppression; Joanna: Herodian court; others unnamed) mirrors the diversity of those who receive the kingdom throughout Luke: no single social category exhausts its reach.
1.christology: The women follow Jesus who proclaims the kingdom — and the kingdom's arrival in their lives preceded the following: they follow because they have already been healed. "Mary, called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out" — her discipleship is the living evidence of the Isaiah 61 program that Jesus announced at Nazareth: the captives released, the oppressed set free. Seven demons corresponds to the most complete demonic oppression possible (seven as the number of completeness); her complete liberation is the complete gift of the one from whom it came. She and the other women are not background figures but the narrative's continuous thread: present at the mission's Galilee beginning and at the resurrection's Jerusalem completion.

4.synthesis: As a great crowd gathers and people from town after town come to Jesus, he speaks in a parable: "A sower went out to sow his seed. And as he sowed, some fell along the path and was trampled underfoot, and the birds ate it up. And some fell on the rock, and as it grew up, it withered away because it had no moisture. And some fell among thorns, and the thorns grew up with it and choked it. And some fell into good soil and grew and yielded a hundredfold" (vv.5–8). The disciples ask about the parable's meaning; Jesus explains that the seed is the word of God. The path hearers have the devil take the word away; the rocky hearers believe for a while and in time of testing fall away; the thorn hearers are choked by cares and riches and pleasures of life; the good-soil hearers hear the word, hold it fast in an honest and good heart, and bear fruit with patience (vv.9–15).
4.historical_context: Palestinian agriculture practiced broadcast sowing before plowing, so seed would naturally land on mixed terrain: the hardened path between fields, thin-soiled limestone outcroppings, weed-infested ground, and the deep alluvial soil of good fields. The hundredfold yield in good soil (v.8) was extraordinary — typical Mediterranean yields were fifteenfold to thirtyfold at best; the hundredfold evokes the eschatological abundance of Genesis 26:12 (Isaac's hundredfold harvest). Luke's interpretation of the parable adds characteristically Lukan emphases: the thorns include "pleasures of life" (hēdonai tou biou) alongside cares and riches (not in Matthew or Mark) — a Lukan note on the danger of wealth-driven distraction. The good soil is described as an "honest and good heart" (kardia kalē kai agathē) — a characteristically Greek formulation (the kalos kagathos ideal) used in a Jewish-Christian context.
4.literary_structure: The parable of the Sower functions as a meta-parable: it is a parable about how parables are received, and about how the word of God is received more broadly. The private interpretation (vv.9–15) frames the four soil types as four descriptions of human response to the word, not four types of people as fixed categories — the same person can shift between responses. The central function of the parable in Luke 8 is to frame everything that follows (the calming of the storm, the Gerasene exorcism, the Jairus/hemorrhaging woman episodes) as demonstrations of the word's power and the challenges to receiving it: the same word that the crowds press to hear is the word that Satan will try to steal, tribulation will try to wither, and anxiety will try to choke.
4.theological_application: "They are those who, hearing the word, hold it fast in an honest and good heart, and bear fruit with patience" (v.15). The three elements of productive hearing are: retaining the word (holding it fast), the quality of the receiving heart (honest and good), and the virtue of persistence (patience — hypomonē). Fruit-bearing is not immediate in the parable; the hundredfold requires a growing season. Luke's insertion of "patience" points to the long-term nature of discipleship: the kingdom does not arrive all at once, and neither does the fruit of the word in a human life. The cares and riches and pleasures that choke (v.14) are all time-pressing competitors — patience is the virtue that keeps the word from being displaced by urgency.
4.christology: "The seed is the word of God" (v.11). The one sowing the word is Jesus, whose entire Galilean mission has been the broadcasting of the kingdom's word across the mixed terrain of human hearts. Luke's fourth Gospel parallel is John 1:1: the Word that Jesus sows is himself — he is simultaneously the Sower and the Word, the one who speaks the kingdom and the kingdom's content. The Sower's extravagance (seed scattered on all soils, including paths and rocks) is the logic of grace: the word is sown without prior assessment of the soil's receptivity, making the grace of the offer universal even as the fruitfulness depends on the heart's response.

16.synthesis: "No one after lighting a lamp covers it with a jar or puts it under a bed, but puts it on a stand so that those who enter may see the light. For nothing is hidden that will not be made manifest, nor is anything secret that will not be known and come to light" (vv.16–17). "Take care then how you hear, for to the one who has, more will be given, and from the one who has not, even what he thinks that he has will be taken away" (v.18). Jesus's mother and brothers arrive, unable to reach him due to the crowd. He is told; he replies: "My mother and my brothers are those who hear the word of God and do it" (vv.19–21).
16.historical_context: The lamp-on-the-stand saying links the private explanation of the Sower parable to the public dimension of the word's proclamation: what has been explained privately to the disciples is intended for public manifestation. The "measure" saying (v.18) echoes Luke 6:38's economy of reciprocity — here applied to receptive hearing: the one who hears well and retains the word will grow in understanding; the one who hears poorly will lose even what seemed to be comprehension. The arrival of Jesus's family (vv.19–21) creates the occasion for one of his most striking redefinitions: the family of God is constituted not by biological kinship but by hearing and doing the word of God. This is not a rejection of his biological family but a redefinition of the primary community.
16.literary_structure: The lamp-on-stand saying (vv.16–17) transitions from the private (parable interpretation for disciples) to the public (disclosure of what is hidden), then to the receptive hearing command (v.18), then to the true-family saying (vv.19–21). The common thread through these three short units is the word of God: the lamp is the word now lit and placed for all to see; the hearing command addresses how one receives the word; the family definition identifies those who hear and do it as Jesus's true community. The true family of Jesus is the community of the fourth good-soil type: honest-hearted, patient bearers of fruit.
16.theological_application: "My mother and my brothers are those who hear the word of God and do it" (v.21). The community gathered around Jesus's word is the family of God — a claim that both expands and redefines kinship. In a culture where family was the foundational social unit of identity, security, and obligation, Jesus's redefinition is radical: the primary family is the hearing-and-doing community, not the biological one. This has been interpreted as the foundation of the church's self-understanding as family: brothers and sisters in Christ are not metaphors but descriptions of a covenant relationship constituted by shared response to the word.
16.christology: The lamp saying in context is christological: the light hidden to be revealed, the secret known and coming to light, is Jesus himself — his identity, hidden in the messianic ministry, will be fully disclosed at the resurrection and the Spirit's coming. The parable interpretation given privately to disciples (v.9-10) will become public proclamation. The Sower, the Lamp, and the redefined family all converge: Jesus sows the word, the word is the light, and the community that forms around hearing and doing the word is his family. To hear the word of God is to hear him; to do it is to be his mother and brother.

22.synthesis: "One day he got into a boat with his disciples, and he said to them, 'Let us go across to the other side of the lake.' So they set out, and as they sailed he fell asleep. And a windstorm came down on the lake, and they were filling with water and were in danger" (vv.22–23). They come and wake him: "Master, Master, we are perishing!" He wakes and rebukes the wind and the raging waves; they cease and there is a calm (v.24). He says to them: "Where is your faith?" They are afraid and marvel, saying to one another: "Who then is this, that he commands even winds and water, and they obey him?" (v.25). The storm stilling is a theophany in miniature: the one asleep in the boat demonstrates divine authority over the sea that the OT consistently reserves for YHWH.
22.historical_context: The Sea of Galilee is subject to sudden violent storms generated by the pressure-differential between the Mediterranean and the Jordan Rift Valley; storms can arise in minutes with waves of several feet. The disciples include experienced fishermen (Peter, Andrew, James, John) accustomed to these waters — their terror suggests the storm was genuinely extreme. Luke addresses Jesus as "Master" (epistata, a term unique to Luke in the Gospels) rather than Mark/Matthew's "Teacher" or "Lord" — a term of respect for a superior authority. The rebuking of the wind and water (v.24) uses the same word (epitimaō) as the rebuke of demons — wind and sea are addressed as personal antagonists whose resistance is commanded into submission.
22.literary_structure: The crossing of the lake initiates the journey to the Gentile region (Gerasenes on the eastern shore), connecting the storm with the territorial advance of the mission into non-Jewish territory. The storm functions narratively as demonic resistance to this advance — the same verb used for exorcism (rebuke) applied to the storm strengthens this reading. The disciples' question — "Who then is this?" (v.25) — is the central question of the entire chapter: answered by the Sower parable (one whose word produces abundant fruit), the lamp (whose secrets are being disclosed), and now this: the one whom winds and water obey.
22.theological_application: "Where is your faith?" (v.25). The disciples have Jesus in the boat and still expect to perish. Faith is not primarily a cognitive exercise but a relational reality: it is the confidence that the one present with them is adequate to the threat. The disciples' failure of faith in the storm prefigures their failure at the Passion — they will again be in extreme danger and will again respond with fear rather than trust. "Where is your faith" is the question the Passion will ask more starkly: will you trust the one who appears to be sleeping, who appears to be dead, to wake and to rise?
22.christology: "Who then is this, that he commands even winds and water, and they obey him?" (v.25). The OT's answer: YHWH, who stilled the sea at the Exodus (Psalm 106:9), who commands the storm and the waves (Psalm 107:29), who "rules the raging of the sea" (Psalm 89:9). The disciples' question is the Gospel's most direct prompt for the answer: this is the Lord of creation, the one through whom all things were made, in whom the fullness of divine authority over the natural order is embodied. Jesus asleep in the stern and Jesus commanding the storm are the same person in two registers: the genuinely human one who grows tired, and the genuinely divine one whom the elements obey.

26.synthesis: They sail to the country of the Gerasenes, opposite Galilee, and are met by a man from the city who has had demons for a long time. He wears no clothes, lives not in a house but among the tombs (vv.26–27). He falls before Jesus and cries out: "What have you to do with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg you, do not torment me" (v.28). Jesus commands the unclean spirit to come out; he asks its name: "Legion" — for many demons had entered him (vv.29–30). They beg not to be sent to the abyss but into a nearby herd of pigs; Jesus permits it; the demons enter the pigs, which rush into the lake and drown (vv.31–33). The herdsmen report to the city; people come and find the man clothed and in his right mind at Jesus's feet — and are afraid (vv.34–37). The man begs to go with Jesus; Jesus sends him away: "Return to your home, and declare how much God has done for you" (vv.38–39).
26.historical_context: The Gerasenes/Gergesenes region (the text-critical variant Gadara also appears) was Gentile territory east of the Jordan and the Sea of Galilee in the Decapolis — a league of Hellenistic cities. The presence of a pig herd confirms the Gentile setting (pigs were non-kosher, not kept in Jewish territory). "Legion" as the demon-name is the Roman military term for approximately 6,000 soldiers — a name that evokes both the number of demons and, for occupied Palestine, the Roman military presence that defined Gentile power. The tombs-dwelling and nakedness mark the man as socially dead and ritually unclean under Jewish law — the ultimate outsider. His restoration to «clothed and in his right mind» (v.35) mirrors the full restoration of a human being: physical, social, and mental wholeness.
26.literary_structure: The Gerasene exorcism is the most dramatic exorcism account in the Synoptics, structured as: identification (vv.26–29) → negotiation (vv.30–33) → community response (vv.34–37) → commission (vv.38–39). The community's fear and request for Jesus to leave (v.37) mirrors the demons' earlier request not to be sent away: both are inappropriate responses to divine power. The man's request to stay with Jesus and Jesus's counter-commission to "declare how much God has done for you" (v.39) results in the man proclaiming "how much Jesus had done for him" — an implicit equation of Jesus with God. The Gerasene becomes the first missionary to the Decapolis Gentile region, sent by Jesus to those Jesus himself does not yet visit.
26.theological_application: The demonic Legion's entry into the pigs and their destruction in the lake is a vivid enacted parable: the demonic power that colonized a human being (made in God's image) is transferred to animals (who bear no image) and is destroyed. The price paid for one human being's restoration is a herd of pigs — the kingdom assigns that value to the image-bearing person. The community's request for Jesus to leave after the restoration is perhaps the Gospel's starkest example of what the Sower parable calls thorns: the community valued its economic stability (pigs) over the restoration of the man, and preferred the absence of the one who healed him to his continued presence among them.
26.christology: "What have you to do with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God?" (v.28). The demon's full christological title — Son of the Most High God — exceeds what most human characters have yet confessed in Luke. The centurion used "Lord"; the crowd called Jesus a great prophet; the disciples ask "who is this?" The demons know. The title "Most High God" (Hypsistos) was used in Jewish and Hellenistic contexts for the supreme God, often by Gentiles acknowledging the Jewish God's transcendence — making its use in Gentile territory particularly resonant. The demons' knowledge of Jesus does not save them: possession of accurate theology is not faith. The man at Jesus's feet, restored, commissioned, and proclaiming — this is the faith the demons lack.

40.pericope_label: Jairus's Daughter and a Sick Woman
40.synthesis: When Jesus returns, the crowd welcomes him. Jairus, a ruler of the synagogue, falls at his feet and begs Jesus to come to his house — his only daughter, about twelve years old, is dying (vv.40–42). As Jesus goes, the crowds press him. A woman who has suffered a discharge of blood for twelve years, spending all her living on physicians to no avail, comes up behind Jesus and touches the fringe of his garment; her bleeding stops immediately (vv.43–44). Jesus asks: "Who was it that touched me?" — the disciples are puzzled by the question given the crowd; the woman falls before him trembling and declares why she touched him and her immediate healing. Jesus: "Daughter, your faith has made you well; go in peace" (vv.45–48). Someone from Jairus's house arrives: the daughter is dead. Jesus: "Do not fear; only believe, and she will be well." At the house, amid weeping mourners, he says: "Do not weep, for she is not dead but sleeping." They laugh. He takes her hand: "Child, arise." Her spirit returns; she stands up at once (vv.49–55).
40.historical_context: The Markan sandwich structure (Jairus's daughter / hemorrhaging woman / Jairus's daughter) is preserved in Luke. The twelve-year parallel — the girl's age and the woman's twelve years of illness — connects the two healings numerically: the woman has suffered for the whole of the girl's life. A ruler of the synagogue (archisunagōgos) was a community leader, making Jairus a figure of religious establishment; his falling at Jesus's feet and begging marks a social reversal. The hemorrhaging woman's condition rendered her ritually unclean under Leviticus 15:25–27 and socially isolated — touching others transferred impurity. Her touch of Jesus's fringe (kraspedon/tzitzit) follows the principle that touching a holy object transfers holiness rather than the reverse. Jesus's question "who touched me" is not about information (he knows) but about summoning the woman to full personal encounter rather than anonymous healing.
40.literary_structure: The double healing is structured to highlight escalating stakes: the woman's twelve-year illness (restored to social life) is interrupted by the news that the girl has died (the boundary that appears final). The transition — "while he was still speaking" — emphasizes the simultaneity of the woman's healing and the child's death-notification. Jesus's "only believe" (v.50) is addressed to Jairus, whose faith is being tested by the intervening death in a way that the hemorrhaging woman's faith was not. The mourners' laughter at Jesus's claim that the girl is sleeping (v.53) is the crowd's version of the disciples' question: who is this who speaks of death as sleep and summons the dead back?
40.theological_application: "Daughter, your faith has made you well; go in peace" (v.48). The woman's anonymous touch in the crowd is insufficient for Jesus — he seeks her out for a personal encounter, public acknowledgment, and direct blessing. The healing through the garment could have remained private; Jesus makes it public and personal: she becomes "daughter" (the same relational word he will use for no one else in Luke), she goes in peace, her faith is acknowledged. This is the fullness of the encounter that the physical healing only begins: healing of body, restoration of relational identity (daughter), public vindication, and commissioning to peace. The anonymous, ashamed, impure woman becomes in one sentence the daughter of the one who healed her.
40.christology: "Do not fear; only believe, and she will be well" (v.50). Jesus speaks this after the death-notification: "only believe" now means believing across the boundary of death. His taking the girl's hand and commanding "Child, arise" (v.54) is the same gesture and genre as the Nain raising — the Lord of life commanding life across death's boundary. That he tells the parents to give her something to eat (v.55) is a detail of bodily realism: the resurrection is not a disembodied spiritual event but the return of full physical life, requiring normal sustenance. Luke's two resurrections (Nain and Jairus) together demonstrate what the answer to John's question (7:22: "the dead are raised") means in practice: the same one who calmed the storm and cast out Legion raises the dead — the Son of the Most High, as the Gerasene demons knew, exercising authority over the final frontier.
